pattern: A printer lays down one solid-colored rectangle per pass, later passes covering earlier ones; decide whether a given color grid could have been produced that way.
hints: Whichever color was printed first must occupy a solid rectangle in the final grid, since nothing was there yet to interrupt its shape.

For each color, compute the smallest bounding rectangle containing all of its cells; if every cell inside that rectangle is either that color or a color you've already confirmed could have been printed even earlier, that color could have gone first (or right after those).
Repeatedly peel off any color whose bounding rectangle is fully explained this way; if a full pass peels off nothing but cells remain, the grid is impossible to print.

edge_inputs.description: the entire grid is a single color, trivially printable in one pass
a single row with two colors side by side
one color's rectangle nests entirely inside another's, requiring two clean passes
several disjoint single-cell colors sit inside one background rectangle
two colors interleave so neither one's bounding rectangle is ever clean, so no print order exists
a color's bounding rectangle contains a third color that itself cannot be isolated first

stress.description: small grids built from a handful of nested rectangles so a valid print order always exists
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    dim = rng.randint(4, min(size, 10))
    grid = [[1] * dim for _ in range(dim)]
    next_color = 2
    layers = rng.randint(1, 4)
    for _ in range(layers):
        r0 = rng.randint(0, dim - 1)
        r1 = rng.randint(r0, dim - 1)
        c0 = rng.randint(0, dim - 1)
        c1 = rng.randint(c0, dim - 1)
        for r in range(r0, r1 + 1):
            for c in range(c0, c1 + 1):
                grid[r][c] = next_color
        next_color += 1
    return [grid]
stress.note: each layer overwrites a random sub-rectangle with a fresh color, mimicking an actual sequence of printer passes
